title: Agent Planning and Task Decomposition
text: A deep research agent benefits from explicit planning because complex questions often contain multiple subproblems. A planner decomposes the user question into search, reading, synthesis, verification, and repair tasks. The plan should expose dependencies so the runtime can execute independent tasks concurrently while blocking dependent tasks until evidence is available.

title: DAG Orchestration for Research Workflows
text: Directed acyclic graphs are useful for research workflows because they represent task dependencies without imposing a single linear order. Topological batches allow safe parallel execution. Cycle detection prevents impossible plans, and blocked status propagation makes failures visible instead of silently skipping work.

title: Asyncio and Semaphore Control
text: Python asyncio can run many IO-heavy agent tasks concurrently. A semaphore limits the number of simultaneous search, read, or model calls. This protects local resources and external APIs while preserving throughput for independent tasks.

title: SQLite Shared Memory for Agents
text: SQLite provides a lightweight persistent memory layer for local agent systems. Runs, tasks, evidence, claims, reports, and agent events can be stored in relational tables. This makes debugging and reproducible experiments easier because every claim can be traced back to evidence and source chunks.

title: Embedding Retrieval with Numpy
text: A vector index stores dense or hashed embeddings and retrieves similar evidence with cosine similarity. Numpy is sufficient for a small educational index. Hybrid retrieval combines lexical matching with vector similarity to reuse useful evidence from prior runs.

title: TextRank Context Compression
text: TextRank scores sentences by building a sentence similarity graph and running a PageRank-style iteration. In deep research, compression should keep high-salience sentences while preserving quoted evidence needed for citation checks. Compression that drops the cited quote can make later verification unreliable.

title: Citation Tracking and Claim Binding
text: Generated research reports are more trustworthy when important claims are bound to inspectable citations. A citation id should point to a concrete evidence object, which points to a source document, chunk, and quote span. This allows a verifier or human reader to audit the report.

title: Hallucination Detection in Research Agents
text: Hallucination detection checks whether claims are supported by retrieved evidence. Unsupported claims, missing citations, and contradictions should be surfaced with a reason. A simple first verifier can combine citation presence, lexical overlap, and contradiction heuristics before using an LLM verifier.

title: Red-Blue Adversarial Repair
text: A Red Agent attacks a draft report along factuality, logic, citation quality, and omissions. A Blue Agent repairs the report using explicit actions such as ADD, DELETE, MODIFY, and VERIFY. The repair log should keep before and after text so improvements are auditable.

title: LLM-as-Judge Evaluation
text: LLM-as-Judge can score generated reports along factuality, coverage, citation quality, structure, and usefulness. The judge should be treated as one metric rather than absolute truth. Offline mock judges are useful for deterministic tests before real model backends are enabled.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Bootstrap confidence intervals estimate uncertainty by repeatedly resampling observed metric values. For agent evaluation, a 95 percent confidence interval helps communicate whether an improvement is stable or just noise from a small benchmark.

title: Cohen's d for Effect Size
text: Cohen's d measures the standardized difference between two sets of scores. It complements p-values and confidence intervals by describing practical effect size. Agent experiments can compare baseline and improved pipelines with Cohen's d.

title: ResearchBench Style Datasets
text: A small ResearchBench style dataset should include questions, gold points, required sources, prohibited claims, and difficulty. It should cover factual explanation, technical comparison, multi-hop reasoning, risk analysis, and solution design.

title: HotpotQA Style Multi-Hop Reasoning
text: HotpotQA style questions require combining evidence from multiple sources. In a research agent, multi-hop tasks test whether the system can connect planner decomposition, retrieval, synthesis, and verification rather than answer from a single snippet.

title: LLM Backend Abstraction
text: A stable LLM backend interface separates agent logic from model providers. Mock, OpenAI-compatible, DeepSeek-compatible, and vLLM-compatible backends can share complete and structured_complete methods. API keys should come from environment variables, not code.

title: Structured Outputs for Agent Systems
text: Structured outputs make agent systems easier to test and debug. Plans, evidence, claims, findings, repair actions, and evaluation results should be represented as typed objects that can be serialized to JSON.

title: Failure Modes in Deep Research Agents
text: Common failure modes include shallow planning, duplicate evidence, unsupported claims, stale memory, over-compression, and judge bias. A reliable system makes these failures visible through statuses, traces, and evaluation reports.

title: Technical Blogging for Engineering Projects
text: A strong technical blog explains the problem, design choices, implementation details, experiments, failures, and next steps. For internship preparation, blog posts should connect code decisions to measurable outcomes and interview explanations.

title: Resume Alignment for Agent Projects
text: A credible resume project should avoid inflated claims. Each bullet should point to working code, experiment results, and a clear explanation. Metrics such as citation coverage and hallucination rate should come from reproducible evaluation scripts.

title: Offline First Agent Development
text: Offline-first development reduces dependency on network, API cost, and model nondeterminism. Mock models, local corpora, and deterministic benchmarks let engineers build orchestration, memory, compression, and evaluation before adding real providers.

title: SQLite and Vector Retrieval Tradeoffs
text: SQLite and vector retrieval solve different parts of an agent memory problem. SQLite is strong for durable records, relational queries, audit trails, and exact traceability from runs to tasks, evidence, claims, reports, and events. Vector retrieval is strong for semantic similarity, fuzzy recall, and reusing evidence when a new query is related but does not share exact keywords. A practical DeepResearch Agent often combines both: SQLite stores canonical records and the vector index stores ids for similarity search.

title: Vector Database Strengths and Limitations
text: Vector databases and vector indexes are useful when semantic retrieval is more important than exact matching. They rank chunks by embedding similarity and can recover related evidence across paraphrases. Their limitations include weaker exact auditing, sensitivity to embedding quality, extra indexing cost, and the need to keep vector ids synchronized with source records. For small offline projects, a numpy index can teach cosine similarity before introducing a production vector database.

title: Hybrid Memory Design for Research Agents
text: A hybrid memory design separates source-of-truth storage from retrieval acceleration. SQLite keeps structured tables for runs, tasks, evidence, claims, reports, verification traces, and repair actions. A vector index stores embeddings keyed by evidence ids and returns candidates for semantic recall. The coordinator can retrieve from the vector index, load full evidence from SQLite, and still preserve citation traceability. This design avoids treating vectors as a replacement for auditable storage.
